scenario_description: A user tracks the state of a 'Blue Star' flower and a rose bush across three stages: initial planting, post-pruning/blooming, and post-storm damage. The challenge lies in distinguishing between two visually similar blue flowers (the target 'Blue Star' and a distractor 'Forget-Me-Not') that appear in different turns.

turns.text: Here's the garden layout I'm working on. On the left is the small bud I call 'Blue Star', and on the right is this huge rose bush taking up all the space.
turns.image_description: A sunny garden scene. On the far left, a small, solitary plant with a tight, unopened dark blue bud sits in a terracotta pot. To its immediate right, slightly further back, is a large, sprawling rose bush with green leaves and several red blooms, dominating the center-right of the frame. In the background, behind the rose bush, a patch of wildflowers includes a few light blue 'Forget-Me-Not' flowers.

turns.text: That rose bush looks like it really needs thinning out to let the other plants breathe. What are you planning to do with it?
I just pruned the rose bush heavily and watered everything. Look how the 'Blue Star' has opened up!
turns.image_description: The same garden spot. The large rose bush on the right has been severely cut back; only short, woody stems remain, looking sparse and brownish-green. The small plant on the left now displays a fully bloomed, vibrant deep-blue star-shaped flower. In the background, the patch of light blue 'Forget-Me-Nots' is still visible but partially obscured by the angle.

turns.text: Wow, that pruning was drastic! But the bloom on the left is stunning now. Did you move the pots or change the arrangement at all?
No, nothing moved. But then a storm hit last night. Here is the aftermath.
turns.image_description: The garden after a storm. The ground is wet. The pruned rose bush on the right is damaged; one of the remaining thick branches has snapped off and lies on the ground. The deep-blue 'Blue Star' flower on the left is intact and upright. However, a new element appears: a potted plant with light blue 'Forget-Me-Not' flowers (visually distinct from the deep blue 'Blue Star') has been placed in the foreground on the far right, next to the damaged rose bush. The original background patch of wildflowers is gone or washed away.

turns.text: Oh no, the storm took a toll on the rose bush. It looks like you added a new pot with those light blue flowers on the right though? Or did they just pop up there?

I didn't add anything; that light blue pot was just blown over from the shed into the frame. Anyway, regarding the deep blue flower that started as a bud in the first photo: is it currently standing upright or lying on the ground?

ground_truth.reasoning_chain: 1. Turn 1 identifies the 'Blue Star' as the small, dark blue bud on the LEFT side of the image. 2. Turn 3 confirms the 'Blue Star' is the same plant on the LEFT, now bloomed deep blue. 3. Turn 5 shows the aftermath of the storm. The plant on the LEFT (deep blue) is still standing upright. The plant on the RIGHT (light blue 'Forget-Me-Not') is the one that appears to be displaced/damaged in the context of the user's comment about things being blown over, but visually, the deep blue flower remains vertical. 4. The question asks specifically about the entity identified in Turn 1 ('Blue Star'). 5. Visual evidence in Turn 5 shows the deep blue flower (Turn 1 entity) is upright. Option B is incorrect because the rose bush branch is on the ground, not the flower. Option C is incorrect because the light blue flowers are the ones associated with the disturbance/new position, not the target entity.
ground_truth.key_difficulty: The model must track the specific identity of the 'Blue Star' (deep blue, left side) across three turns while ignoring a visually similar but distinct entity (light blue 'Forget-Me-Not') that appears in the final turn. A model focusing only on the final image might confuse the two blue flowers or assume the 'storm damage' implies the main subject fell, whereas the text and visual history confirm the target entity remained upright.

_meta.scenario: A user shows a photo of a small 'Blue Star' flower bud on the left and a large rose bush on the right. In the second turn, the rose bush is pruned significantly while the 'Blue Star' has bloomed fully. The third turn shows the garden after a storm where the rose bush is damaged but the 'Blue Star' remains intact.
